explain.rule: The past simple describes completed actions in the past. For regular verbs, add -ed to the base verb. For questions, use did + subject + base verb. For negatives, use didn't + base verb. The verb never changes for any person — I worked, you worked, he worked, we worked, they worked.
explain.full_explanation: The past simple is used for any action that started and finished in the past. It does not matter if the action happened one second ago or one hundred years ago — if it is finished and in the past, use past simple. The key signal is that there is usually a time expression: yesterday, last week, in 2015, when I was young, an hour ago.

positive: All persons: subject + verb+ed → I worked, you walked, he talked, she visited, we finished, they started
negative: All persons: subject + didn't + base verb → I didn't work, she didn't go, they didn't finish
question: All persons: Did + subject + base verb? → Did you work? Did she go? Did they finish?
short_answers: Yes, I did. / No, I didn't.
Yes, she did. / No, she didn't.

spelling_rules: Most verbs: add -ed → work→worked, talk→talked, start→started, finish→finished
Verbs ending in -e: add only -d → live→lived, love→loved, arrive→arrived, use→used
Verbs ending in consonant + y: change y to i, add -ed → study→studied, try→tried, carry→carried
Verbs ending in vowel + y: just add -ed → play→played, enjoy→enjoyed, stay→stayed
Short verbs ending in one vowel + one consonant: double the consonant, add -ed → stop→stopped, plan→planned, drop→dropped, travel→travelled (UK), traveled (US)
when_to_use: Completed actions at a specific time in the past: I visited my grandmother last Sunday.
A series of completed past actions: She woke up, had breakfast, and left the house.
Past habits that no longer happen (with used to or just past simple): As a child, I walked to school every day.
Stories and narratives: The story started in a small village in the north of England.
Historical facts with specific times: Neil Armstrong walked on the moon in 1969.
time_expressions: last night, last week, last month, last year, last Tuesday
ago: two hours ago, three days ago, a week ago, five years ago
in + year: in 2010, in 1995, in the 1980s
when I was young / a child / a student
at + time: at seven o'clock, at midnight
on + day: on Monday, on my birthday
in + month/season: in January, in the summer
explain.why_matters: The past simple is the main tense for telling stories and describing past events. Any time you talk about something that happened — a holiday, your childhood, what happened yesterday, news events, history — you need the past simple. It is one of the most used tenses in English storytelling and conversation.
explain.hebrew_note: The past simple is quite familiar to Hebrew speakers because Hebrew also has a clear past tense that describes completed actions. The main differences are: (1) In English, the verb form is the same for ALL persons — I worked, she worked, they worked. There is no feminine or plural distinction in the verb itself. (2) English uses 'did' to make questions and 'didn't' to make negatives — Hebrew uses a different structure. (3) The word order in English questions is: Did + subject + base verb? You cannot say 'You worked yesterday?' as a proper question.
explain.tip: Remember: in past simple, 'did' does all the work. Once you use did or didn't, the main verb goes back to its simplest base form. Did you work? She didn't work. Did they arrive? Never say 'Did you worked?' or 'She didn't worked' — the -ed is in did, not in the main verb.

common_errors.reason: After did, the verb returns to base form — no -ed
After didn't, the verb returns to base form

common_errors.wrong: I have visited Paris last year.
common_errors.reason: Specific past time expressions (last year) always use past simple, not present perfect
'Yesterday' signals past tense — the verb needs -ed
Short verb ending in one vowel + one consonant: double the consonant — stop→stopped

good_examples.sentence: I visited my grandparents last weekend.
good_examples.audio_text: I visited my grandparents last weekend.
good_examples.note: Completed action with time expression 'last weekend'

good_examples.sentence: She studied English for three hours yesterday.
good_examples.audio_text: She studied English for three hours yesterday.

good_examples.note: Question with did — enjoy stays as base verb
Negative with didn't — arrive stays as base verb

good_examples.sentence: We moved to Tel Aviv three years ago.
good_examples.audio_text: We moved to Tel Aviv three years ago.
good_examples.note: move→moved — verb ending in -e, add only -d

good_examples.sentence: The concert started at eight and finished at eleven.
good_examples.audio_text: The concert started at eight and finished at eleven.
good_examples.note: Two completed past actions in sequence

good_examples.sentence: When did you last travel abroad?
good_examples.audio_text: When did you last travel abroad?
good_examples.note: Wh-question with did — travel stays as base verb

good_examples.sentence: He stopped the car and stepped out.
good_examples.audio_text: He stopped the car and stepped out.
good_examples.note: stop→stopped — short verb, double the consonant

bad_examples.reason: After did, the main verb must be base form — no -ed
After didn't, the main verb must be base form

bad_examples.wrong: I have visited Paris last year.
bad_examples.reason: 'Last year' is a specific past time — always use past simple, never present perfect
Short verb (one vowel + one consonant at the end): double the consonant before -ed
'Yesterday' signals that this is past — the verb must have -ed

exercises.question: She ___ (visit) her aunt in Haifa last Saturday.
exercises.hint: Regular verb in the past → add -ed
exercises.explanation: Visit is a regular verb. Past simple = visited. Time expression 'last Saturday' confirms past tense.

exercises.question: I ___ (not / watch) television last night — I read a book.
exercises.hint: Past negative → didn't + base verb (no -ed!)
exercises.explanation: Past negatives use didn't + base verb. Watch stays as 'watch' — not 'watched'.
exercises.hebrew_note: בעברית: 'לא צפיתי' — הפועל מוטה. באנגלית: didn't watch — הפועל נשאר בצורת הבסיס

exercises.question: ___ (you / enjoy) the concert last night?
exercises.hint: Past question → Did + subject + base verb
exercises.explanation: Past questions: Did + subject + base verb. Enjoy stays as 'enjoy' after did — not 'enjoyed'.

exercises.question: They ___ (move) to a new apartment two months ago.
exercises.hint: Move ends in -e → add only -d
exercises.explanation: Verbs ending in -e just add -d: move→moved, love→loved, live→lived.

exercises.question: He ___ (study) for the exam all night.
exercises.hint: Study ends in consonant + y → change y to i, add -ed
exercises.explanation: Study → studied. Consonant (d) + y: change y to i, add -ed. Other examples: try→tried, carry→carried.

exercises.question: We ___ (not / finish) the project on time.
exercises.hint: Past negative → didn't + base verb
exercises.explanation: Didn't + base verb. Finish stays as 'finish' — not 'didn't finished'.

exercises.question: The rain ___ (stop) at about five o'clock.
exercises.hint: Stop ends in one vowel + one consonant → double the consonant, add -ed
exercises.explanation: Stop → stopped. Short verb rule: one vowel (o) + one consonant (p) at the end → double the final consonant.

exercises.question: When ___ (the meeting / start)?
exercises.hint: When question in the past → When + did + subject + base verb
exercises.explanation: Wh-questions in the past: question word + did + subject + base verb. Start stays as 'start'.

exercises.question: She ___ (play) the piano when she was a child.
exercises.hint: Play ends in vowel + y → just add -ed
exercises.explanation: Play ends in vowel (a) + y → just add -ed: played. Compare: study (consonant + y) → studied.

exercises.question: I ___ (not / like) vegetables when I was young, but now I do.
exercises.hint: Past negative + like ends in -e → base form is 'like'
exercises.explanation: Didn't + base verb. Like ends in -e but that doesn't matter here — after didn't the verb is always base form.

exercises.question: The children ___ (arrive) at school very early this morning.
exercises.hint: Arrive ends in -e → add only -d
exercises.explanation: Arrive → arrived. Ends in -e, so add just -d.

exercises.question: ___ (they / travel) to Europe for their holiday last summer?
exercises.hint: Past question → Did + subject + base verb
exercises.explanation: Did + they + travel. Travel is the base form — not 'travelled' after did.

exercises.question: Choose the correctly formed past simple sentence:
exercises.answer: She finished her report an hour ago.
exercises.hint: Which sentence follows all the rules for past simple?
exercises.explanation: 'Finished' is correct — finish + ed. 'An hour ago' confirms past. The other options have errors in question/negative formation.
distractors: She finished her report an hour ago? (wrong punctuation for statement)
She did finished her report an hour ago.
She finish her report an hour ago.

exercises.question: Which question is correctly formed?
exercises.answer: Did you call your mother yesterday?
exercises.hint: Past question = Did + subject + base verb
exercises.explanation: 'Did you call' is correct. After did, the verb must be base form. 'Called' after did is wrong. 'You called yesterday?' has no did.
distractors: Did you called your mother yesterday?
Do you call your mother yesterday?

exercises.question: They ___ the game because they played very well.
exercises.hint: Think about the meaning — what is the natural verb here? Note: this is an irregular verb preview.
exercises.explanation: 'Won' is the past of 'win'. This is an irregular verb — it will be covered fully in G-03. For now, notice that past simple is used here because the game is over.

exercises.question: I ___ (not) to the party because I was tired.
exercises.hint: Past negative → didn't + base verb
exercises.explanation: 'Didn't go' is correct. Go is the base verb. 'Didn't went' is a very common error — after didn't, NEVER use the past form.

exercises.question: The correct spelling of the past simple of 'plan' is:
exercises.hint: Plan ends in one vowel + one consonant — what is the spelling rule?
exercises.explanation: Plan → planned. Short verb: one vowel (a) + one consonant (n) → double the final consonant before -ed.

exercises.question: Which time expression tells you to use past simple?
exercises.hint: Which expression places the action firmly in the past at a specific time?
exercises.explanation: 'Three days ago' is a specific completed past time → past simple. 'Since' and 'already' are associated with present perfect (G-15). 'Now' signals present.

exercises.question: Find and fix the mistake: 'Did you enjoyed the party last night?'
exercises.answer: Did you enjoy the party last night?
exercises.hint: What happens to the main verb after did in a question?
exercises.explanation: After did, the main verb returns to its base form. 'Enjoyed' → 'enjoy'. Did already shows the past tense.
exercises.hebrew_note: זהו טעות נפוצה מאוד — 'Did you enjoyed' טבעי ללומדים כי הם רוצים לסמן עבר פעמיים. אבל did כבר עושה זאת.

exercises.question: Find and fix the mistake: 'She didn't finished her homework before dinner.'
exercises.answer: She didn't finish her homework before dinner.
exercises.hint: What form does the main verb take after didn't?
exercises.explanation: Didn't + base verb. 'Finish' is the base form — not 'finished'. The -ed ending is already in 'didn't'.

exercises.question: Find and fix the mistake: 'Yesterday I work from nine to six.'
exercises.answer: Yesterday I worked from nine to six.
exercises.hint: 'Yesterday' is a past time expression — what does the verb need?
exercises.explanation: 'Yesterday' confirms this is a past action. The verb needs -ed: work → worked.

exercises.question: Find and fix the mistake: 'We stoped at a cafe on the way home.'
exercises.answer: We stopped at a cafe on the way home.
exercises.hint: Stop ends in one vowel + one consonant — what is the spelling rule?
exercises.explanation: Stop → stopped. One vowel (o) + one consonant (p) → double the final consonant before -ed.

exercises.question: Find and fix the mistake: 'I have visited London last year.'
exercises.hint: 'Last year' is a specific past time — which tense does it need?
exercises.explanation: Specific past time expressions (last year, yesterday, in 2019, two weeks ago) always require past simple, not present perfect.
exercises.hebrew_note: דוברי עברית לעיתים משתמשים בצורה מורכבת יותר כשהם לא בטוחים. אבל עם 'last year' — תמיד past simple

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: Did you enjoy the film last night?
exercises.hint: Past question: Did + subject + base verb + object + time
exercises.explanation: Did + you + enjoy (base verb) + the film (object) + last night (time). Never 'Did you enjoyed'.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.hint: Past negative: subject + didn't + base verb + object
exercises.explanation: Subject (she) + didn't + base verb (answer) + object (her phone).

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.hint: Subject + past verb + time expression with 'ago'
exercises.explanation: Positive past: subject + verb+ed + time. 'Ago' always goes at the end of the time expression.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: When did the television programme start?
exercises.hint: Wh-question: when + did + subject + base verb
exercises.explanation: When + did + the television programme (subject) + start (base verb). Note: no -ed after did.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: She studied her notes all evening.
exercises.hint: Positive past: subject + studied + object + time. Study→studied (consonant+y rule).
exercises.explanation: She + studied + her notes + all evening. Study→studied because it ends in consonant (d) + y.

speak.sentence: I visited my grandparents in Haifa last weekend.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Visited' = VIZ-it-id. Three syllables. The final -ted sounds like -tid. Don't say viz-IT-ed with stress on the middle syllable.

speak.sentence: Did you enjoy the concert last night?
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Concert' = KON-sert. Stress on first syllable. 'Enjoy' = en-JOY. Stress on second syllable. In a question, your voice rises slightly at the end.

speak.sentence: She didn't finish her work until very late.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Didn't' = DID-unt. Say quickly as one word. 'Finish' = FIN-ish. 'Until' = un-TIL. Stress on second syllable.

speak.sentence: We moved to a new house three years ago.
chunks: We moved to a new house
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Moved' — the 'oo' sound like in 'food'. 'Three' — practice the TH sound: put your tongue between your teeth. 'Ago' = uh-GOH. Stress on second syllable.

speak.sentence: He studied very hard for his exams but still failed.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Studied' = STUD-eed. Two syllables. 'Exams' = ig-ZAMZ. Stress on second syllable. 'Failed' = FAYLD. The 'ai' sounds like the 'a' in 'cake'.

speak.sentence: When did you last travel outside of Israel?
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Travel' = TRAV-ul. Two syllables, stress on first. 'Outside' = owt-SYD. Stress on second syllable. 'Israel' = IZ-ree-ul. Three syllables.

speak.sentence: The meeting started at nine and finished at half past twelve.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Started' = STAR-tid. 'Finished' = FIN-isht. The final -ed sounds like a T here. 'Twelve' = TWELV. Don't forget the L sound before V.

speak.sentence: I didn't sleep well last night because it was too hot.
chunks: I didn't sleep well last night
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Sleep' — the 'ee' is long, like in 'feet'. 'Because' = bih-KOZ. Stress on second syllable. 'Hot' — short O sound, like in British English 'not'.
